posts.name: Nicolo Trevisani
posts.text:
Dear combine experts,
I am trying to extract the charge asymmetry in the WH production.
To do so, I am defining a physics model, and I would ask your help in understanding if what I am doing is correct. In particular, I often get that the uncertainty on the asymmetry is as large as the ‘parameter range’ I define in combine. Trying to understand what causes this behavior, I observed that the negative log likelihood quickly reaches a plateau and stops increasing its value when I perform a scan versus the asymmetry value.
I tried to describe my approach and summarize the combine commands I use and the corresponding results in a set of slides:

docs.google.com

Fit_sanity_checks_in_WH_analysis

Fit sanity checks in WH analysis Nicolò Trevisani KIT - Karlsruhe Institute of Technology 17/11/2022

Please let me know if anything is unclear or if you have any suggestions or feedback.
Best regards,
Nicolò
posts.cooked_html:
Dear combine experts,

I am trying to extract the charge asymmetry in the WH production.

To do so, I am defining a physics model, and I would ask your help in understanding if what I am doing is correct. In particular, I often get that the uncertainty on the asymmetry is as large as the ‘parameter range’ I define in combine. Trying to understand what causes this behavior, I observed that the negative log likelihood quickly reaches a plateau and stops increasing its value when I perform a scan versus the asymmetry value.

I tried to describe my approach and summarize the combine commands I use and the corresponding results in a set of slides:

docs.google.com

Fit_sanity_checks_in_WH_analysis

Fit sanity checks in WH analysis Nicolò Trevisani KIT - Karlsruhe Institute of Technology 17/11/2022

Please let me know if anything is unclear or if you have any suggestions or feedback.

Best regards,
Nicolò

posts.name: Adinda de Wit
posts.text:
Dear Nicolò,
To answer the question on slide 9: yes, there is a substantial difference between what you show on slide 8 and slide 9: in slide 8, r_S and r_higgs are floating while on slide 9 they are fixed. On slide 8 you didn’t specify explicitly that r_S and r_higgs should float freely, but they do as they don’t have a constraint in the workspace. You would obtain the same result by using --redefineSignalPOIs r_higgs,r_S,r_A -P r_A --floatOtherPOIs 1 (and this would be the recommended way to ensure the other POIs are really floating)
As to why the dNLL plateaus - to me it looks like for extreme values of the asymmetry, the WH cross section is taken almost down to 0 (you can save the value of r_S in the output tree with --trackParameters r_S). For r_S=0 you get deltaNLL ~0.25, and at this point you stop depending on r_A   - so I think what you are seeing is consistent.
Best,
Adinda
posts.cooked_html:
Dear Nicolò,

To answer the question on slide 9: yes, there is a substantial difference between what you show on slide 8 and slide 9: in slide 8, r_S and r_higgs are floating while on slide 9 they are fixed. On slide 8 you didn’t specify explicitly that r_S and r_higgs should float freely, but they do as they don’t have a constraint in the workspace. You would obtain the same result by using --redefineSignalPOIs r_higgs,r_S,r_A -P r_A --floatOtherPOIs 1 (and this would be the recommended way to ensure the other POIs are really floating)

As to why the dNLL plateaus - to me it looks like for extreme values of the asymmetry, the WH cross section is taken almost down to 0 (you can save the value of r_S in the output tree with --trackParameters r_S). For r_S=0 you get deltaNLL ~0.25, and at this point you stop depending on r_A - so I think what you are seeing is consistent.

Best,
Adinda

posts.name: Nicolo Trevisani
posts.text:
Dear Adinda,
thank you for the helpful clarifications!
I tried repeating the scan with the commands you suggested and I get - as expected - the same results.
Also regarding the reason of the plateaus, I think you are right. In addition, we realized that by definition, the asymmetry is limited to (-1,1) so it makes sense that we don’t see any usual behavior of the likelihood beyond that range.
Best regards,
Nicolò
posts.cooked_html:
Dear Adinda,

thank you for the helpful clarifications!

I tried repeating the scan with the commands you suggested and I get - as expected - the same results.

Also regarding the reason of the plateaus, I think you are right. In addition, we realized that by definition, the asymmetry is limited to (-1,1) so it makes sense that we don’t see any usual behavior of the likelihood beyond that range.

Best regards,
Nicolò
